data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-02T01:35:41.871Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating a .NET 10 web API to .NET 11. The app uses EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider.
We have a repository pattern where some methods use synchronous LINQ:

```csharp
public class ProductRepository
{
    private readonly CosmosDbContext _context;

    public List<Product> GetAll()
    {
        return _context.Products.ToList();
    }

    public Product GetById(int id)
    {
        return _context.Products.First(p => p.Id == id);
    }

    public int GetCount()
    {
        return _context.Products.Count();
    }

    public void Save(Product product)
    {
        _context.Products.Add(product);
        _context.SaveChanges();
    }
}
```

In .NET 10 we had the sync-over-async opt-in configured. What breaks in .NET 11?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about what breaks when migrating from .NET 10 to .NET 11 with EF Core using the Azure Cosmos DB provider, specifically regarding synchronous LINQ operations.

Let me think about what I know about this topic:

In EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider, there has been a progression of changes regarding synchronous operations:

1. In EF Core 9 (.NET 9), the Cosmos DB provider started throwing exceptions for synchronous operations by default, because the underlying Azure Cosmos DB SDK is async-only. Sync calls were doing "sync-over-async" which is problematic (deadlocks, thread pool starvation).


2. In .NET 10 (EF Core 10), an opt-in mechanism was introduced that allowed developers to suppress the exception and use sync-over-async behavior through configuration, though this remained a workaround rather than a supported pattern.
3. In .NET 11 (EF Core 11), that opt-in mechanism would likely be removed entirely, forcing all Cosmos DB operations to be fully asynchronous with no fallback to synchronous calls.

Actually, let me reconsider what's actually documented. EF Core 9 already removed sync support from the Cosmos provider and throws an exception by default, with only a temporary opt-in available. Since the user is migrating from .NET 10 to .NET 11 and mentions having the opt-in configured, the most likely scenario is that this temporary workaround is being removed in the newer version, requiring a full migration to async patterns.
